title: Vitamin A
content: Vitamin A (also known as Retinol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports vision (especially night vision); immune function; skin health; cell growth. Food sources: Liver, egg yolks, dairy, carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach, kale, mango. Adult RDA: 900 mcg RAE (men) / 700 mcg RAE (women). Deficiency symptoms: Night blindness, dry eyes, dry skin, frequent infections. Deficiency disease(s): Night blindness / Xerophthalmia.

title: Vitamin B1
content: Vitamin B1 (also known as Thiamin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Helps convert food into energy; supports nerve and muscle function. Food sources: Whole grains, pork, legumes, seeds, nuts, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 1.2 mg (men) / 1.1 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, irritability, nerve damage, muscle weakness. Deficiency disease(s): Beriberi.

title: Vitamin B2
content: Vitamin B2 (also known as Riboflavin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports energy production; cell function; growth; helps break down fats and drugs. Food sources: Dairy, eggs, lean meats, green vegetables, fortified grains. Adult RDA: 1.3 mg (men) / 1.1 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Cracked lips, sore throat, skin disorders, eye fatigue. Deficiency disease(s): Ariboflavinosis.

title: Vitamin B3
content: Vitamin B3 (also known as Niacin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports digestive system; skin; nerves; helps convert food to energy. Food sources: Meat, fish, poultry, whole grains, peanuts, legumes. Adult RDA: 16 mg (men) / 14 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, skin rashes, digestive issues, mental confusion. Deficiency disease(s): Pellagra.

title: Vitamin B5
content: Vitamin B5 (also known as Pantothenic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Helps synthesize coenzyme A for fatty acid metabolism; supports hormone production. Food sources: Chicken, beef, potatoes, oats, tomatoes, whole grains, mushrooms. Adult RDA: 5 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, irritability, numbness, tingling in hands/feet. Deficiency disease(s): Rare - Paresthesia.

title: Vitamin B6
content: Vitamin B6 (also known as Pyridoxine) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports brain development; immune function; amino acid metabolism. Food sources: Poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 1.3 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Anemia, skin rashes, confusion, weakened immunity. Deficiency disease(s): Peripheral neuropathy.

title: Vitamin B7
content: Vitamin B7 (also known as Biotin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports metabolism of fats; carbohydrates; proteins; supports hair and nail health. Food sources: Eggs, nuts, seeds, salmon, sweet potatoes, organ meats. Adult RDA: 30 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Hair thinning, brittle nails, skin rashes. Deficiency disease(s): Biotin deficiency (rare).

title: Vitamin B9
content: Vitamin B9 (also known as Folate / Folic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Essential for DNA synthesis and cell division; critical during pregnancy for fetal development. Food sources: Leafy greens, legumes, citrus fruits, asparagus, fortified grains. Adult RDA: 400 mcg DFE. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, poor concentration, birth defects if deficient in pregnancy. Deficiency disease(s): Folate-deficiency anemia.
keywords: Vitamin B9; Folate / Folic Acid; Vitamin

title: Vitamin B12
content: Vitamin B12 (also known as Cobalamin) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports red blood cell formation; neurological function; DNA synthesis. Food sources: Meat, fish, dairy, eggs, fortified nutritional yeast (not found in plant foods). Adult RDA: 2.4 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, numbness/tingling, memory problems, pale skin. Deficiency disease(s): Pernicious anemia / B12-deficiency anemia.

title: Vitamin C
content: Vitamin C (also known as Ascorbic Acid) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Antioxidant; supports immune function; collagen synthesis; iron absorption; wound healing. Food sources: Citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, tomatoes, kiwi. Adult RDA: 90 mg (men) / 75 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Bleeding gums, slow wound healing, joint pain, easy bruising. Deficiency disease(s): Scurvy.

title: Vitamin D
content: Vitamin D (also known as Calciferol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Supports calcium absorption; bone health; immune and muscle function. Food sources: Fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms; also produced via sun exposure. Adult RDA: 600-800 IU (15-20 mcg). Deficiency symptoms: Bone pain, muscle weakness, increased fracture risk. Deficiency disease(s): Rickets (children) / Osteomalacia (adults).

title: Vitamin E
content: Vitamin E (also known as Tocopherol) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Antioxidant; protects cells from damage; supports immune function and skin health. Food sources: Nuts, seeds, vegetable oils, spinach, avocado. Adult RDA: 15 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, vision problems, immune issues (rare, usually from fat malabsorption). Deficiency disease(s): Vitamin E deficiency (rare).

title: Vitamin K
content: Vitamin K (also known as Phylloquinone / Menaquinone) is a vitamin. Key benefits: Essential for blood clotting and bone metabolism. Food sources: Leafy greens (kale, spinach), broccoli, Brussels sprouts, fermented foods. Adult RDA: 120 mcg (men) / 90 mcg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Easy bruising, excessive bleeding, poor bone health. Deficiency disease(s): Vitamin K deficiency bleeding.

title: Calcium
content: Calcium is a mineral. Key benefits: Builds and maintains bones and teeth; supports muscle function; nerve signaling; blood clotting. Food sources: Dairy products, leafy greens, fortified plant milks, tofu, almonds, sardines. Adult RDA: 1000-1200 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle cramps, brittle nails, numbness, increased fracture risk. Deficiency disease(s): Osteoporosis / Osteopenia.

title: Iron
content: Iron is a mineral. Key benefits: Essential for hemoglobin production and oxygen transport in the blood. Food sources: Red meat, poultry, seafood, lentils, beans, spinach, fortified cereals. Adult RDA: 8 mg (men) / 18 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, pale skin, weakness, shortness of breath, cold hands/feet. Deficiency disease(s): Iron-deficiency anemia.

title: Magnesium
content: Magnesium is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports muscle and nerve function; blood sugar regulation; blood pressure; bone health. Food sources: Nuts, seeds, whole grains, leafy greens, legumes, dark chocolate. Adult RDA: 400-420 mg (men) / 310-320 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Muscle cramps, fatigue, irregular heartbeat, numbness. Deficiency disease(s): Hypomagnesemia.

title: Zinc
content: Zinc is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports immune function; wound healing; DNA synthesis; taste/smell. Food sources: Meat, shellfish, legumes, seeds, nuts, dairy. Adult RDA: 11 mg (men) / 8 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Hair loss, delayed wound healing, weakened immunity, loss of appetite. Deficiency disease(s): Zinc deficiency.

title: Potassium
content: Potassium is a mineral. Key benefits: Regulates fluid balance; muscle contractions; nerve signals; supports heart function. Food sources: Bananas, potatoes, beans, spinach, avocado, oranges. Adult RDA: 2600-3400 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, cramps, irregular heartbeat, fatigue. Deficiency disease(s): Hypokalemia.

title: Sodium
content: Sodium is a mineral. Key benefits: Regulates fluid balance and blood pressure; supports nerve and muscle function. Food sources: Table salt, processed foods, bread, cheese, canned soups. Adult RDA: 1500-2300 mg (upper limit). Deficiency symptoms: Nausea, muscle cramps, confusion, low blood pressure (deficiency is rare). Deficiency disease(s): Hyponatremia.

title: Phosphorus
content: Phosphorus is a mineral. Key benefits: Works with calcium to build bones and teeth; supports energy storage and cell membrane structure. Food sources: Meat, dairy, fish, nuts, legumes, whole grains. Adult RDA: 700 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Bone pain, weakness, loss of appetite (deficiency is rare). Deficiency disease(s): Hypophosphatemia.

title: Iodine
content: Iodine is a mineral. Key benefits: Essential for thyroid hormone production; which regulates metabolism. Food sources: Iodized salt, seaweed, fish, dairy, eggs. Adult RDA: 150 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Goiter, fatigue, weight gain, developmental delays in infants. Deficiency disease(s): Iodine-deficiency disorder / Goiter.

title: Selenium
content: Selenium is a mineral. Key benefits: Antioxidant; supports thyroid function and immune health. Food sources: Brazil nuts, seafood, meat, eggs, sunflower seeds. Adult RDA: 55 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Muscle weakness, fatigue, weakened immunity, thyroid problems. Deficiency disease(s): Keshan disease (rare).

title: Copper
content: Copper is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports iron metabolism; connective tissue formation; nervous system function. Food sources: Shellfish, nuts, seeds, whole grains, organ meats, dark chocolate. Adult RDA: 900 mcg. Deficiency symptoms: Fatigue, pale skin, weakened immunity, brittle bones. Deficiency disease(s): Copper deficiency (rare).

title: Manganese
content: Manganese is a mineral. Key benefits: Supports bone formation; metabolism; antioxidant defense. Food sources: Whole grains, nuts, legumes, tea, leafy vegetables. Adult RDA: 2.3 mg (men) / 1.8 mg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Bone malformation, weakness, impaired growth (rare). Deficiency disease(s): Manganese deficiency (rare).

title: Chromium
content: Chromium is a mineral. Key benefits: Enhances insulin action and supports carbohydrate/fat metabolism. Food sources: Broccoli, whole grains, meat, nuts, brewer's yeast. Adult RDA: 35 mcg (men) / 25 mcg (women). Deficiency symptoms: Impaired glucose tolerance, unintended weight loss (rare). Deficiency disease(s): Chromium deficiency (rare).

title: Fluoride
content: Fluoride is a mineral. Key benefits: Strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent tooth decay. Food sources: Fluoridated water, tea, seafood, fluoride toothpaste. Adult RDA: 3-4 mg. Deficiency symptoms: Increased risk of tooth decay. Deficiency disease(s): Dental caries risk.

title: Innate vs Adaptive Immunity
content: The immune system has two main branches. Innate immunity is the body's fast, non-specific first line of defense, active from birth, including physical barriers (skin, mucus), and cells like neutrophils and macrophages that attack any invader. Adaptive immunity is slower to activate but highly specific and creates memory of past pathogens, driven mainly by T-cells and B-cells.
keywords: innate immunity; adaptive immunity; immune system overview; first line of defense

title: Helper T-cells (CD4+)
content: Helper T-cells (CD4+ T-cells) coordinate the immune response by releasing cytokines that activate other immune cells, including B-cells and cytotoxic T-cells. They do not kill infected cells directly but act as the 'command center' of adaptive immunity. HIV specifically targets and depletes CD4+ helper T-cells, which is why it severely weakens immune function.
keywords: helper T cells; CD4; cytokines; adaptive immunity; T lymphocytes

title: Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+)
content: Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+ T-cells) directly kill cells that are infected with viruses or that have become cancerous. They recognize infected cells via antigen fragments displayed on the cell surface (MHC class I molecules) and release enzymes that induce cell death in the target cell.
keywords: cytotoxic T cells; CD8; killer T cells; MHC class I; viral infection

title: Regulatory T-cells (Tregs)
content: Regulatory T-cells (Tregs) suppress and control the immune response to prevent it from attacking the body's own tissues, and to shut the response down once an infection is cleared. Dysfunction in Tregs is linked to autoimmune diseases.
keywords: regulatory T cells; Tregs; immune tolerance; autoimmune disease

title: B-cells and Antibody Production
content: B-cells are white blood cells responsible for producing antibodies (immunoglobulins). When a B-cell encounters an antigen it recognizes, with help from helper T-cells it differentiates into plasma cells that mass-produce antibodies specific to that antigen, and memory B-cells that allow a faster response if the same pathogen returns.
keywords: B cells; antibodies; immunoglobulins; plasma cells; memory B cells

title: Types of Antibodies
content: There are five main antibody classes: IgG (most abundant, provides long-term immunity and crosses the placenta), IgA (found in mucosal secretions like saliva and breast milk), IgM (first antibody produced during an infection), IgE (involved in allergic reactions and parasite defense), and IgD (function still not fully understood, found on B-cell surfaces).
keywords: IgG; IgA; IgM; IgE; IgD; antibody classes; immunoglobulin types

title: Types of White Blood Cells (Leukocytes)
content: White blood cells are grouped into granulocytes and agranulocytes. Granulocytes include neutrophils (most abundant, first responders that engulf bacteria), eosinophils (fight parasites and involved in allergic responses), and basophils (release histamine during allergic reactions). Agranulocytes include lymphocytes (T-cells, B-cells, natural killer cells) and monocytes, which mature into macrophages that engulf pathogens and debris.
keywords: white blood cells; leukocytes; neutrophils; eosinophils; basophils; monocytes; macrophages

title: Primary vs Secondary Immune Response
content: The primary immune response occurs the first time the body encounters a pathogen and takes days to build up, while producing memory cells. The secondary immune response occurs on re-exposure to the same pathogen; because memory B-cells and T-cells already exist, the response is much faster and stronger. Vaccines work by safely triggering a primary response so the body is ready with a fast secondary response if it meets the real pathogen later.
keywords: primary immune response; secondary immune response; immunological memory; vaccination

title: How Nutrition Supports Immune Function
content: Several nutrients are directly required for immune cell production and function: Vitamin C supports white blood cell function and acts as an antioxidant; Vitamin D modulates both innate and adaptive immune responses; zinc is essential for the development and function of neutrophils and natural killer cells; and protein provides the building blocks for antibodies. Chronic deficiency in any of these can impair immune response and increase infection risk.
keywords: nutrition and immunity; vitamin C immunity; vitamin D immunity; zinc immunity; protein immunity

title: Vegetarian Diet Overview
content: A vegetarian diet excludes meat, poultry, and fish but typically includes dairy and eggs (lacto-ovo vegetarian). It emphasizes vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and seeds. Key nutritional considerations include ensuring adequate iron, vitamin B12 (mainly from dairy/eggs), zinc, and complete protein through combining grains and legumes.
keywords: vegetarian diet; lacto-ovo vegetarian; plant-based; iron; vitamin B12

title: Non-Vegetarian Diet Overview
content: A non-vegetarian diet includes meat, poultry, fish, and seafood alongside plant foods. It naturally provides complete protein, heme iron (more easily absorbed than plant-based iron), vitamin B12, and omega-3 fatty acids from fish. Balance and moderation matter: excessive red or processed meat intake is associated with higher cardiovascular risk, so lean meats, poultry, and fish are generally favored over processed meats.
keywords: non-vegetarian diet; heme iron; complete protein; omega-3; lean meat

title: Ketogenic (Keto) Diet
content: The ketogenic diet is a very low-carbohydrate, high-fat, moderate-protein diet (typically around 5-10% carbs, 70-75% fat, 15-20% protein) designed to shift the body into ketosis, where it burns fat for fuel instead of glucose. It was originally developed to manage epilepsy and is also used for weight loss. Common foods include meat, fatty fish, eggs, butter, nuts, and low-carb vegetables, while grains, sugar, and most fruits are avoided. It requires medical supervision for people with diabetes or kidney conditions.
keywords: ketogenic diet; keto; low carb; ketosis; high fat diet

title: Mediterranean Diet
content: The Mediterranean diet is based on traditional eating patterns of countries bordering the Mediterranean Sea. It emphasizes olive oil as the primary fat source, vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and fish, with moderate dairy and poultry and limited red meat. It is widely studied and associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, largely due to its high content of monounsaturated fats, fiber, and antioxidants.
keywords: mediterranean diet; olive oil; heart health; monounsaturated fat; fiber

title: Immunity-Boosting Diet Principles
content: An immunity-supportive diet focuses on nutrient density rather than any single 'superfood.' Key priorities include: adequate protein for antibody production; vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, bell peppers, broccoli); vitamin D (fatty fish, fortified foods, sun exposure); zinc (seeds, legumes, meat); probiotic/fermented foods (yogurt, kefir) for gut-immune health; and limiting added sugar and excessive alcohol, both of which can suppress immune cell function.
keywords: immunity boosting diet; immune supportive foods; gut health; probiotics; nutrient density

title: Vegan Diet Overview
content: A vegan diet excludes all animal products, including dairy, eggs, and honey, relying entirely on plant foods. It requires deliberate planning to avoid deficiencies in vitamin B12 (not naturally present in plant foods and typically requires fortified foods or supplementation), iron, calcium, omega-3 fatty acids, and sometimes protein, depending on food variety and intake.
keywords: vegan diet; plant-based; vitamin B12 supplementation; calcium; omega-3 ALA

title: Scurvy
content: Scurvy is caused by prolonged vitamin C (ascorbic acid) deficiency. Vitamin C is required to synthesize collagen, so deficiency weakens connective tissue, leading to bleeding gums, easy bruising, joint pain, poor wound healing, and fatigue. It is treated and prevented with adequate intake of citrus fruits, bell peppers, and other vitamin C-rich foods.
keywords: scurvy; vitamin C deficiency; collagen; bleeding gums; ascorbic acid

title: Anemia
content: Anemia is a condition where the blood lacks enough healthy red blood cells or hemoglobin to carry adequate oxygen. Nutritional anemia is most commonly caused by iron deficiency (iron-deficiency anemia), but can also result from vitamin B12 deficiency (pernicious/megaloblastic anemia) or folate deficiency. Symptoms include fatigue, pale skin, shortness of breath, and dizziness.
keywords: anemia; iron deficiency anemia; B12 deficiency; folate deficiency; hemoglobin

title: Rickets
content: Rickets is a childhood disease caused by vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate deficiency, resulting in softening and weakening of bones. It leads to skeletal deformities such as bowed legs, delayed growth, and bone pain. The adult equivalent, caused by the same deficiencies, is called osteomalacia. Prevention includes adequate vitamin D from sunlight, fortified foods, and fatty fish.
keywords: rickets; vitamin D deficiency; osteomalacia; bone softening; calcium

title: Beriberi
content: Beriberi is caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency. 'Wet beriberi' affects the cardiovascular system, causing swelling and heart failure, while 'dry beriberi' affects the nervous system, causing muscle weakness, nerve damage, and difficulty walking. It is historically linked to diets based heavily on polished (white) rice with little else.
keywords: beriberi; thiamine deficiency; vitamin B1; wet beriberi; dry beriberi

title: Pellagra
content: Pellagra is caused by niacin (vitamin B3) deficiency, often associated with diets heavily reliant on untreated corn/maize. It is classically described by the '4 Ds': dermatitis (skin rash on sun-exposed areas), diarrhea, dementia, and if untreated, death. Treatment involves niacin supplementation and dietary diversification.
keywords: pellagra; niacin deficiency; vitamin B3; dermatitis; four Ds

title: Goiter and Iodine Deficiency Disorders
content: Iodine deficiency impairs the thyroid gland's ability to produce thyroid hormone, causing the gland to enlarge (goiter) as it tries to compensate. Severe deficiency during pregnancy can cause irreversible cognitive impairment in the child (cretinism). Iodized salt and seafood are the main dietary sources used to prevent it.
keywords: iodine deficiency; goiter; thyroid; cretinism; iodized salt

title: Kwashiorkor and Marasmus
content: Kwashiorkor and marasmus are severe forms of protein-energy malnutrition, most common in children in food-insecure regions. Kwashiorkor results primarily from protein deficiency despite adequate calorie intake, causing edema (swelling), a distended belly, and fatty liver. Marasmus results from severe overall calorie and nutrient deficiency, causing extreme wasting of muscle and fat with no edema.
keywords: kwashiorkor; marasmus; protein energy malnutrition; edema; child malnutrition

title: Night Blindness / Xerophthalmia
content: Vitamin A deficiency impairs the retina's ability to adapt to low light, causing night blindness, and can progress to xerophthalmia (severe dryness of the eyes), corneal damage, and in advanced untreated cases, irreversible blindness. It remains a leading cause of preventable childhood blindness in developing countries.
keywords: vitamin A deficiency; night blindness; xerophthalmia; retinol; childhood blindness

title: Turmeric and Curcumin
content: Turmeric is a spice containing curcumin, a compound with notable anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Research suggests curcumin may help reduce markers of inflammation and support joint health, though curcumin has poor bioavailability on its own and is often paired with black pepper (piperine) to improve absorption. It is commonly used in cooking and as a supplement.
keywords: turmeric; curcumin; anti-inflammatory; antioxidant; piperine; bioavailability

title: Ginger
content: Ginger is a root widely used for its anti-nausea and digestive benefits, including relief from motion sickness and pregnancy-related nausea. It contains gingerol, a bioactive compound with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, and is also commonly used to help ease muscle soreness and support digestion.
keywords: ginger; gingerol; nausea relief; digestion; anti-inflammatory

title: Garlic
content: Garlic contains allicin, a sulfur compound formed when garlic is crushed or chopped, which is associated with cardiovascular benefits such as modestly lower blood pressure and cholesterol, as well as antimicrobial properties. Regular consumption is also studied for potential immune-supportive effects, though effect sizes in human studies vary.
keywords: garlic; allicin; cardiovascular health; antimicrobial; cholesterol

title: Green Tea
content: Green tea is rich in catechins, particularly EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), a powerful antioxidant. It is associated with modest benefits for metabolism, cardiovascular health, and cellular protection against oxidative stress. It also contains a moderate amount of caffeine along with L-theanine, which together are thought to produce a calmer form of alertness compared to coffee.
keywords: green tea; EGCG; catechins; antioxidant; L-theanine

title: Berries (Blueberries, Strawberries)
content: Berries are dense in anthocyanins and other polyphenol antioxidants, which help combat oxidative stress linked to aging and chronic disease. They are also a good source of fiber and vitamin C relative to their calorie content, and are commonly associated with cognitive and cardiovascular health benefits in observational studies.

title: Yogurt and Fermented Foods
content: Fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, and kimchi contain live probiotic bacteria that support gut microbiome diversity. Since a large portion of the immune system is closely tied to gut health, regular intake of fermented foods is associated with improved digestion and may support immune function indirectly through the gut-immune axis.
keywords: yogurt; probiotics; fermented foods; gut microbiome; gut-immune axis

title: High-Protein Diet Plan
content: A high-protein diet emphasizes protein at every meal to support muscle building, recovery, satiety, and weight management. Target roughly 1.2-2.0 g of protein per kg of body weight depending on activity. Best protein sources: chicken breast, fish, eggs, Greek yogurt, lentils, chickpeas, tofu, and whey. Sample day: eggs and Greek yogurt at breakfast; grilled chicken or lentil bowl at lunch; fish or tofu with vegetables at dinner; nuts or cottage cheese as snacks. Pair protein with fibre-rich vegetables and whole grains for balance. Good for: muscle gain, active individuals, satiety during weight loss. Keep saturated fat moderate by favouring lean and plant proteins.
keywords: high protein; muscle building; protein rich; lean protein; chicken; fish; eggs; lentils; recovery; satiety

title: Low-Fat Diet Plan
content: A low-fat diet limits total and saturated fat to support heart health, weight management, and conditions needing reduced fat intake. Keep fat to roughly 20-30% of calories and minimize fried and processed foods. Emphasize: vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, lean poultry, white fish, and non-fat dairy. Cook by baking, steaming, grilling, or boiling rather than frying. Sample day: oats with fruit; a large salad with grilled chicken and light dressing; steamed fish with vegetables and brown rice. Use healthy fats (olive oil, nuts) in small controlled amounts rather than eliminating them entirely. Good for: heart health, high cholesterol, gallbladder issues, calorie control.
keywords: low fat; heart healthy; reduced fat; lean; low cholesterol diet; baked; steamed; weight management

title: Low-Carb Diet Plan
content: A low-carb diet reduces carbohydrate intake to help control blood sugar, support weight loss, and steady energy. Limit refined carbs (white bread, sugar, sweets) and moderate starchy foods; get carbs mainly from vegetables and some fruit. Emphasize: protein, non-starchy vegetables, healthy fats, eggs, fish, poultry, nuts, and seeds. Sample day: eggs and avocado; a chicken and vegetable salad; salmon with greens; nuts or cheese as snacks. Especially useful for people managing diabetes or insulin resistance because it blunts blood-sugar spikes. Good for: type 2 diabetes, weight loss, blood-sugar control. Ensure enough fibre from vegetables to aid digestion.
keywords: low carb; blood sugar control; diabetes diet; weight loss; reduced carbohydrate; insulin resistance; keto adjacent

title: Low-Sodium Diet Plan
content: A low-sodium diet limits salt to support healthy blood pressure and reduce strain on the heart and kidneys. Aim for under about 1,500-2,300 mg of sodium per day. Avoid processed foods, canned soups, cured meats, salty snacks, and excess restaurant food. Emphasize: fresh vegetables and fruit, whole grains, unsalted legumes, fresh lean protein, and herbs and spices instead of salt for flavour. Sample day: oatmeal with fruit; a fresh salad with unsalted grilled chicken; home-cooked fish with steamed vegetables seasoned with lemon and herbs. Read labels for hidden sodium. Good for: high blood pressure (hypertension), heart disease, kidney disease.
keywords: low sodium; low salt; blood pressure; hypertension; heart disease; kidney; DASH; fresh foods

title: High-Fibre Diet Plan
content: A high-fibre diet increases dietary fibre to support digestion, gut health, steady blood sugar, and cholesterol control. Aim for roughly 25-38 g of fibre per day from whole foods. Emphasize: whole grains (oats, brown rice, whole wheat), legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas), vegetables, fruit with skin, nuts, and seeds. Increase fibre gradually and drink plenty of water to avoid discomfort. Sample day: oats with berries and chia; a lentil and vegetable bowl; brown rice with beans and greens; fruit and nuts as snacks. Good for: constipation, gut health, high cholesterol, blood-sugar stability, and satiety during weight management.
keywords: high fibre; high fiber; digestion; gut health; whole grains; legumes; cholesterol; constipation; satiety

title: Balanced Diet Plan
content: A balanced diet provides all major nutrients in healthy proportions to maintain overall health, energy, and a healthy weight. Roughly fill half the plate with vegetables and fruit, a quarter with lean protein, and a quarter with whole grains, plus some healthy fats and dairy or alternatives. Emphasize variety, whole and minimally processed foods, and appropriate portions. Sample day: whole-grain breakfast with fruit and yogurt; a mixed plate of lean protein, vegetables, and whole grains at lunch and dinner; fruit, nuts, or yogurt as snacks. Good for: general health maintenance, people without a specific clinical restriction, and building sustainable eating habits.
keywords: balanced diet; general health; variety; portion control; whole foods; maintenance; healthy eating; well rounded

content: Recipe: Sunshine Immunity Smoothie. A vitamin-C-rich breakfast smoothie blending orange, kiwi, and mango with a squeeze of lemon and a spoon of yogurt for probiotics. Calories: 180. A high-protein, high-fiber vegetarian-adaptable bowl, good for muscle recovery and general strength/energy goals.
keywords: High protein, Quinoa, Power Bowl, vegetarian, muscle recovery, fiber, iron

title: Hemoglobin
content: Hemoglobin (also spelled Haemoglobin) is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. Normal levels are roughly 13.5-17.5 g/dL for men and 12.0-15.5 g/dL for women. Low hemoglobin (haemoglobin) causes anemia, leading to fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath. Producing hemoglobin requires adequate iron, vitamin B12, and folate. Foods that help raise hemoglobin: iron-rich foods (spinach, lentils, red meat, beans), vitamin C (improves iron absorption), vitamin B12 and folate sources (eggs, dairy, leafy greens).
keywords: Hemoglobin, Haemoglobin, HGB, anemia, iron, oxygen, red blood cells, blood test

title: Chronic Kidney Disease
content: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is the gradual loss of kidney function over time, most commonly caused by diabetes and high blood pressure. Symptoms often appear late and include fatigue, swelling in the legs/ankles, changes in urination, and nausea. CKD is staged 1-5 based on estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR), with stage 5 being kidney failure. Dietary management for CKD typically involves limiting sodium, potassium, and phosphorus intake, moderating protein intake, and staying within fluid limits as advised by a doctor. Regular blood tests (creatinine, BUN, GFR) are used to monitor kidney health.
keywords: Chronic Kidney Disease, CKD, kidney failure, renal, GFR, creatinine, dialysis, diabetes, blood pressure

title: Vitamin Deficiency Overview
content: Vitamin deficiency occurs when the body doesn't get or absorb enough of a specific vitamin, leading to health problems that range from mild to severe. Common deficiency diseases include Scurvy (vitamin C), Rickets/Osteomalacia (vitamin D), Night Blindness (vitamin A), and Beriberi (vitamin B1). Anemia can result from deficiencies in iron, vitamin B12, or folate. Common causes include poor diet, malabsorption disorders, restrictive diets, and certain medications. General prevention includes eating a varied, balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and adequate protein, with supplementation recommended by a doctor when a specific deficiency is diagnosed via blood tests.
keywords: Vitamin deficiency, malnutrition, Scurvy, Rickets, Night Blindness, Beriberi, Anemia, deficiency disease

title: COVID-19
content: COVID-19 is a respiratory illness. Source: caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, spread mainly through respiratory droplets and close contact with an infected person. Symptoms: fever, cough, fatigue, loss of taste or smell, sore throat, and shortness of breath in severe cases. Prevention: vaccination, wearing masks in crowded indoor spaces, good hand hygiene, and staying home when sick. Nutrition support: adequate protein for tissue repair, vitamin C and zinc for immune support, vitamin D, and staying well hydrated. Rest and medical care are essential; nutrition supports recovery but does not replace treatment.
keywords: COVID-19, COVID, coronavirus disease, SARS-CoV-2, respiratory illness, pandemic

title: Coronavirus
content: Coronaviruses are a family of viruses. Source: spread mainly via respiratory droplets and close contact; the family includes viruses causing the common cold as well as more severe diseases like SARS, MERS, and COVID-19 (caused by SARS-CoV-2). Symptoms: range from mild cold-like symptoms to severe respiratory illness depending on the specific virus. Prevention: hand hygiene, respiratory etiquette (covering coughs/sneezes), avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and vaccination where available. Nutrition support: adequate protein, vitamin C, vitamin D, and zinc, along with good hydration and rest, helps the body recover from viral respiratory infections.
keywords: Coronavirus, virus family, SARS, MERS, respiratory virus

title: Malaria
content: Malaria is a parasitic disease. Source: caused by Plasmodium parasites, transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes (does not spread person-to-person through casual contact). Symptoms: high fever, chills, sweating, headache, nausea, and muscle pain, often occurring in cycles; can cause anemia due to destruction of red blood cells. Prevention: sleeping under insecticide-treated mosquito nets, using mosquito repellent, eliminating standing water where mosquitoes breed, and antimalarial preventive medication for travelers to high-risk areas. Nutrition support: iron and folate-rich foods to help rebuild red blood cells, plenty of fluids during fever, and easily digestible, calorie-dense foods during recovery. Antimalarial medical treatment is essential.
keywords: Malaria, Plasmodium, mosquito-borne disease, fever, anemia

title: Dengue Fever
content: Dengue fever is a viral infection. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, which typically bite during the day and breed in stagnant water. Symptoms: sudden high fever, severe headache, pain behind the eyes, joint and muscle pain, rash, and a drop in platelet count; severe cases can lead to dengue hemorrhagic fever. Prevention: eliminating stagnant water around homes, using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing long sleeves/pants in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: high fluid intake to prevent dehydration, vitamin C rich foods, and protein-rich foods to support platelet and tissue recovery; papaya leaf extract is a widely used traditional remedy, though medical monitoring of platelet counts is essential.
keywords: Dengue, dengue fever, Aedes mosquito, platelet count, viral fever

title: Chikungunya
content: Chikungunya is a viral infection closely related to dengue. Source: spread by the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes. Symptoms: sudden fever and severe joint pain (often the most prominent symptom), muscle pain, headache, and rash; joint pain can persist for weeks or months after the fever resolves. Prevention: eliminating mosquito breeding sites (stagnant water), using mosquito repellent and nets, and wearing protective clothing. Nutrition support: anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3s, turmeric), adequate hydration, and rest during the acute fever phase.
keywords: Chikungunya, chickengunia, chikungunya fever, joint pain, Aedes mosquito

title: Asthma
content: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition. Source: airways become inflamed and narrowed, triggered by allergens, cold air, exercise, respiratory infections, air pollution, or genetic predisposition (it is not contagious). Symptoms: wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. Prevention: identifying and avoiding personal triggers, using prescribed control medication consistently, getting flu/pneumonia vaccinations, and keeping indoor air clean. Nutrition support: an anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids, fruits, and vegetables; adequate vitamin D; and identifying/avoiding specific food triggers if present. Asthma requires ongoing medical management alongside supportive nutrition.
keywords: Asthma, wheezing, chronic respiratory disease, airway inflammation

title: CBC Report (Complete Blood Count)
content: A Complete Blood Count (CBC) is a common blood test. It measures red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), hemoglobin, hematocrit (PCV), and platelets. Use: screens for and helps diagnose conditions like anemia (low hemoglobin/RBC), infections (high WBC), and clotting disorders (abnormal platelets). Prevention/monitoring: routine CBC testing helps catch nutrient deficiencies and blood disorders early, before symptoms become severe. Understanding your CBC report alongside a doctor's interpretation helps identify nutrient deficiencies (like iron or B12 deficiency) that may need dietary correction.
keywords: CBC, complete blood count, blood test, blood report, RBC, WBC, platelets, hemoglobin

title: Tuberculosis (TB)
content: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs, spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Symptoms: persistent cough (sometimes with blood), unexplained weight loss, night sweats, fever, and fatigue. Prevention: BCG vaccination (given in many countries at birth), good ventilation, early detection and treatment of active cases, and completing the full antibiotic course to prevent drug-resistant strains. Nutrition support: TB commonly causes malnutrition and significant weight loss, so a high-protein, high-calorie diet, along with adequate vitamin D and vitamin A, supports immune function and tissue repair during the long antibiotic treatment course.
keywords: Tuberculosis, TB, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, lung infection, persistent cough

title: Chickenpox
content: Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection. Source: caused by the varicella-zoster virus, spread through direct contact with the rash or through respiratory droplets; most common in children. Symptoms: itchy, blister-like rash, fever, tiredness, and loss of appetite. Prevention: varicella vaccination is highly effective; avoiding contact with infected individuals if not immune. Nutrition support: soft, easy-to-swallow foods if there are mouth sores, plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration, and vitamin C rich foods to support healing; avoid scratching to prevent scarring and secondary infection.
keywords: Chickenpox, chicken pox, varicella-zoster, viral rash

title: Jaundice
content: Jaundice is a yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by a buildup of bilirubin in the blood. Source: often signals an underlying liver problem (such as hepatitis), bile duct blockage, or excessive red blood cell breakdown, rather than being a disease itself. Prevention: vaccination against hepatitis, avoiding excess alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight to prevent fatty liver disease, and prompt treatment of underlying conditions. Nutrition support: a liver-friendly diet that's low in fat and avoids alcohol, adequate hydration, and easily digestible, high-fiber foods; the underlying cause needs medical diagnosis and treatment.
keywords: Jaundice, yellowing skin, bilirubin, liver problem

title: Food Poisoning
content: Food poisoning is an illness caused by consuming contaminated food or water. Source: bacteria (like Salmonella, E. coli), viruses, parasites, or their toxins in improperly handled, stored, or cooked food. Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and sometimes fever. Prevention: thorough handwashing, cooking food to safe temperatures, avoiding cross-contamination, and proper refrigeration of perishables. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte fluids to prevent dehydration, a bland diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) as symptoms ease, and probiotics to help restore healthy gut bacteria; seek medical care if symptoms are severe or persistent.
keywords: Food poisoning, foodborne illness, contaminated food, gastroenteritis

title: Hepatitis
content: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver. Source: most often viral infections (Hepatitis A and E spread via contaminated food/water; Hepatitis B, C, and D spread via blood or bodily fluids), though it can also result from alcohol use, toxins, or autoimmune conditions. Symptoms: jaundice, fatigue, abdominal pain, nausea, and dark urine. Prevention: vaccination (available for Hepatitis A and B), safe food/water practices, avoiding sharing needles, and safe sex practices. Nutrition support: a balanced diet with adequate protein for liver repair, avoiding alcohol and fatty/fried foods, staying hydrated, and small frequent meals if appetite is reduced.
keywords: Hepatitis, hepatitis A, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, liver inflammation, viral hepatitis

title: Cholera
content: Cholera is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Vibrio cholerae, usually spread through contaminated drinking water or food, especially where sanitation is poor. Symptoms: severe watery diarrhea and vomiting, which can lead to rapid, life-threatening dehydration. Prevention: access to clean drinking water, proper sanitation, food safety practices, and oral cholera vaccines in high-risk areas. Nutrition support: immediate oral rehydration solution (ORS) with the correct balance of salts and sugar is the most critical intervention, along with zinc supplementation, which has been shown to reduce the duration and severity of diarrhea.
keywords: Cholera, Vibrio cholerae, severe diarrhea, dehydration, waterborne disease

title: Diarrhea
content: Diarrhea is frequent, loose, or watery stools. Source: commonly caused by infections (viral, bacterial, or parasitic), food intolerances, contaminated water/food, or medications. Symptoms: can lead to dehydration and loss of electrolytes if prolonged. Prevention: safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, proper food handling, and avoiding known personal food triggers. Nutrition support: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte drinks, the BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast), probiotics to restore gut bacteria, and temporarily avoiding dairy, fatty, and spicy foods until symptoms resolve.
keywords: Diarrhea, diarrhoea, loose stools, dehydration, gastrointestinal illness

title: Depression
content: Depression is a common mental health condition causing persistent sadness, loss of interest in activities, and difficulty with daily functioning. Source: arises from a combination of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors (e.g. chronic stress, major life changes, trauma); it requires proper diagnosis and treatment by a mental health professional (therapy and/or medication). Prevention: maintaining social connections, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, stress management, and seeking help early when symptoms appear. Nutrition support: diets rich in omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins, vitamin D, and probiotics (supporting the gut-brain axis) are associated with better mood regulation, but nutrition is a complement to professional treatment, not a replacement for it. If you or someone you know is struggling, please reach out to a healthcare provider.
keywords: Depression, mental health, mood disorder, gut-brain axis

title: Typhoid Fever
content: Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection. Source: caused by Salmonella Typhi, spread through food or water contaminated with the feces of an infected person. Symptoms: prolonged high fever, weakness, stomach pain, headache, and loss of appetite. Prevention: typhoid vaccination, safe drinking water, good hand hygiene, and proper sanitation. Nutrition support: a soft, high-calorie, easily digestible diet during recovery (khichdi-style meals, soups, bananas), adequate fluids, and small frequent meals since appetite is often reduced; antibiotic treatment is essential alongside nutritional support.
keywords: Typhoid, typhoid fever, Salmonella Typhi, prolonged fever

title: Flu (Influenza)
content: The flu (influenza) is a contagious viral respiratory infection. Source: spreads mainly through respiratory droplets when infected people cough, sneeze, or talk. Symptoms: fever, chills, muscle aches, cough, sore throat, congestion, and fatigue. Prevention: annual flu vaccination is the best prevention, along with good hand hygiene and avoiding close contact with sick individuals. Nutrition support: vitamin C and zinc rich foods to support immune function, warm fluids and soups (chicken soup has real anti-inflammatory and hydrating benefits), and adequate rest.
keywords: Flu, influenza, viral respiratory infection, seasonal flu

title: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)
content: HIV is a virus that attacks the body's immune system, specifically CD4 (T-helper) cells. Source: spread through contact with certain body fluids of an infected person - unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles/syringes, mother-to-child transmission during pregnancy/birth/breastfeeding, or contaminated blood transfusions; it does not spread through casual contact, saliva, or air. Symptoms: flu-like illness within 2-4 weeks of infection (fever, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes), followed by a long asymptomatic period; without treatment, it progressively weakens the immune system. Prevention: consistent condom use, not sharing needles, pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) medication for high-risk individuals, regular testing, and antiretroviral treatment during pregnancy to prevent mother-to-child transmission. Nutrition support: a high-protein, adequate-calorie diet to maintain weight and muscle mass, micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium) for immune function, and extra food safety care due to increased infection vulnerability.
keywords: HIV, human immunodeficiency virus, CD4 cells, immune system virus

title: AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome)
content: AIDS is the most advanced stage of HIV infection. Source: occurs when HIV has severely damaged the immune system (very low CD4 cell count) if left untreated, leaving the body vulnerable to opportunistic infections and certain cancers; the underlying cause is the same as HIV transmission (unprotected sex, needle sharing, mother-to-child transmission). Symptoms: rapid weight loss, recurring fever, prolonged swollen lymph glands, chronic diarrhea, persistent sores, pneumonia, and memory problems, largely from opportunistic infections the weakened immune system can no longer fight off. Prevention: the same prevention measures as HIV, plus - most importantly - early HIV diagnosis and consistent antiretroviral therapy (ART), which prevents progression from HIV to AIDS almost entirely. Nutrition support: high-protein, high-calorie diet with micronutrient support (vitamin A, C, E, zinc, selenium), strict food safety (avoiding raw/undercooked foods) due to severely weakened immunity, and small frequent meals if appetite is poor.
keywords: AIDS, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome, advanced HIV, opportunistic infections

title: Cancer
content: Cancer is a group of diseases involving abnormal, uncontrolled cell growth that can invade nearby tissue and spread to other parts of the body. Source: caused by genetic mutations in cells, driven by risk factors including tobacco use, excessive alcohol, poor diet, obesity, physical inactivity, UV/radiation exposure, certain infections (e.g. HPV, Hepatitis B/C), family history, and environmental carcinogens. Symptoms: vary widely by type, but general warning signs include unexplained weight loss, persistent fatigue, unusual lumps, changes in skin, a persistent cough, and changes in bowel/bladder habits. Prevention: avoiding tobacco, limiting alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight, regular physical activity, a balanced diet rich in fruits/vegetables/fiber, limiting processed and red meat, protecting skin from UV exposure, HPV and Hepatitis B vaccination, and regular screenings for early detection. Nutrition support: antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables, adequate protein (especially important during treatment), fiber for digestive/colon health, and limiting processed meats and excess alcohol; nutrition can support prevention and recovery but medical treatment is essential.
keywords: Cancer, tumor, malignant cells, oncology, carcinogen

title: Breast Cancer
content: Breast cancer is a cancer that forms in the cells of the breast, most commonly in the ducts or lobules. Source: risk factors include age, family history/genetic mutations (BRCA1/BRCA2), early menstruation/late menopause, obesity, alcohol use, and hormone therapy. Symptoms: a new lump or mass, breast swelling, skin dimpling, nipple discharge or inversion, and breast or nipple pain. Prevention: regular self-exams and mammogram screening for early detection, maintaining a healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular physical activity, and breastfeeding when possible. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and fiber, limiting processed and red meat, and maintaining a healthy weight all help lower risk and support recovery during treatment.
keywords: Breast cancer, mammogram, BRCA, breast lump

title: Lung Cancer
content: Lung cancer is a cancer that begins in the cells of the lungs. Source: the leading cause is tobacco smoking (including secondhand smoke), followed by exposure to radon gas, asbestos, air pollution, and family history. Symptoms: a persistent cough, coughing up blood, chest pain, hoarseness, unexplained weight loss, and shortness of breath. Prevention: not smoking (or quitting), avoiding secondhand smoke, testing homes for radon, and avoiding occupational carcinogen exposure. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables (particularly ones with antioxidants) is associated with lower risk; adequate protein and calories are important to prevent weight loss during treatment.
keywords: Lung cancer, smoking, radon, persistent cough

title: Skin Cancer (Melanoma)
content: Skin cancer is abnormal growth of skin cells, most often on skin exposed to the sun; melanoma is the most serious type. Source: primarily caused by ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sun exposure or tanning beds; fair skin, many moles, and family history increase risk. Symptoms: a new mole or a change in an existing mole's size, shape, or color; a sore that doesn't heal; or a spot that looks different from others. Prevention: using sunscreen, wearing protective clothing, avoiding peak sun hours and tanning beds, and regular skin self-exams for early detection. Nutrition support: antioxidant-rich foods (vitamin C, vitamin E, beta-carotene) may help protect skin cells from UV-related damage, alongside sun-safety practices.
keywords: Skin cancer, melanoma, UV radiation, mole changes

title: Blood Cancer (Leukemia)
content: Leukemia is a cancer of the blood-forming tissues, including bone marrow, that leads to production of abnormal white blood cells. Source: exact cause is often unknown; risk factors include genetic disorders, previous chemotherapy/radiation, exposure to certain chemicals (like benzene), and family history. Symptoms: fatigue, frequent infections, easy bruising or bleeding, fever, and unexplained weight loss. Prevention: no guaranteed prevention exists since causes are largely genetic/unknown, but avoiding exposure to industrial chemicals and radiation when possible may reduce risk. Nutrition support: a high-protein, nutrient-dense diet supports blood cell recovery during treatment; food safety is especially important since chemotherapy weakens the immune system.
keywords: Leukemia, blood cancer, bone marrow, white blood cells

title: Colorectal Cancer
content: Colorectal cancer is cancer that begins in the colon or rectum, often starting as precancerous polyps. Source: risk factors include age, a diet high in red/processed meat and low in fiber, obesity, physical inactivity, smoking, alcohol, and family history/inflammatory bowel disease. Symptoms: changes in bowel habits, blood in stool, persistent abdominal discomfort, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue. Prevention: regular colonoscopy screening after age 45-50 (or earlier with family history) to detect and remove polyps before they become cancerous, along with a high-fiber diet and limiting processed/red meat. Nutrition support: high-fiber foods (whole grains, fruits, vegetables, legumes), calcium, and limiting processed and red meat are associated with lower risk.
keywords: Colorectal cancer, colon cancer, polyps, colonoscopy

title: Prostate Cancer
content: Prostate cancer is cancer that develops in the prostate gland in men. Source: risk increases with age, family history, and is more common in men of African descent; diet and obesity may also play a role. Symptoms: often none in early stages; later symptoms include difficulty urinating, blood in urine or semen, and pelvic discomfort. Prevention: regular PSA blood test and screening discussions with a doctor after age 50 (or earlier with family history), maintaining a healthy weight, and regular physical activity. Nutrition support: a diet rich in vegetables (especially tomatoes/lycopene), limiting red meat and high-fat dairy, and maintaining a healthy weight are associated with lower risk.
keywords: Prostate cancer, PSA test, men's health

title: Cervical Cancer
content: Cervical cancer is cancer that occurs in the cells of the cervix, almost always caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV). Source: HPV infection (sexually transmitted) is the primary cause; smoking and a weakened immune system increase risk. Symptoms: often none in early stages; later symptoms include abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge and pelvic pain. Prevention: HPV vaccination, regular Pap smear/HPV screening for early detection of precancerous changes, and safe sex practices. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables supports overall immune function, which helps the body clear HPV infections before they progress.
keywords: Cervical cancer, HPV, Pap smear, women's health

title: Liver Cancer
content: Liver cancer is cancer that begins in the cells of the liver. Source: chronic Hepatitis B or C infection, cirrhosis (often from alcohol use or fatty liver disease), and aflatoxin exposure (a toxin from mold on stored grains/nuts) are major risk factors. Symptoms: unexplained weight loss, loss of appetite, upper abdominal pain, jaundice, and abdominal swelling. Prevention: Hepatitis B vaccination, avoiding excess alcohol, maintaining a healthy weight to prevent fatty liver disease, and proper food storage to avoid aflatoxin contamination. Nutrition support: a balanced, liver-friendly diet low in fat and alcohol, with adequate protein, supports liver health and recovery.

title: Stomach Cancer
content: Stomach (gastric) cancer is cancer that forms in the lining of the stomach. Source: chronic H. pylori bacterial infection, a diet high in smoked/salted/pickled foods, smoking, and family history are key risk factors. Symptoms: indigestion, stomach pain, unintended weight loss, feeling full after small meals, and nausea. Prevention: treating H. pylori infection, limiting smoked/salted/processed foods, not smoking, and eating plenty of fresh fruits and vegetables. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fresh fruits and vegetables (particularly citrus and leafy greens) and low in salted/smoked/processed foods is associated with lower risk.
keywords: Stomach cancer, gastric cancer, H. pylori

title: Oral Cancer
content: Oral cancer is cancer that develops in any part of the mouth, including the lips, tongue, and gums. Source: tobacco use (smoking or chewing) and heavy alcohol use are the leading causes, especially combined; HPV infection is also a growing risk factor. Symptoms: a mouth sore that doesn't heal, a lump or thickening in the cheek, white or red patches, and difficulty chewing or swallowing. Prevention: avoiding tobacco in all forms, limiting alcohol, HPV vaccination, and regular dental checkups for early detection. Nutrition support: a diet rich in fruits and vegetables supports oral tissue health; avoiding tobacco and excess alcohol remains the most important preventive step.
keywords: Oral cancer, mouth cancer, tobacco, HPV

title: Lymphoma
content: Lymphoma is a cancer of the lymphatic system, part of the body's immune system, categorized broadly into Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Source: exact cause is often unknown; risk factors include a weakened immune system, certain viral infections (like Epstein-Barr virus), and family history. Symptoms: painless swelling of lymph nodes (neck, armpit, groin), fatigue, fever, night sweats, and unexplained weight loss. Prevention: no guaranteed prevention exists, but maintaining a healthy immune system and avoiding known viral risk factors when possible may help. Nutrition support: a nutrient-dense, high-protein diet supports the immune system and helps maintain strength during treatment.
keywords: Lymphoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, lymph nodes, lymphatic system

title: Pancreatic Cancer
content: Pancreatic cancer is cancer that forms in the tissues of the pancreas, an organ involved in digestion and blood sugar regulation. Source: risk factors include smoking, chronic pancreatitis, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and family history. Symptoms: often vague in early stages; later symptoms include abdominal pain radiating to the back, jaundice, unexplained weight loss, and new-onset diabetes. Prevention: not smoking, maintaining a healthy weight, limiting alcohol, and managing chronic pancreatitis or diabetes. Nutrition support: small, frequent, easily digestible meals with adequate protein and calories are important, since the pancreas's digestive role is often affected; a dietitian's guidance is valuable during treatment.

title: Apple
content: Apple is a popular fruit, available in many varieties (red, green, yellow). Key nutrients: fiber (especially pectin), vitamin C, and antioxidants like quercetin, concentrated mostly in the skin. Health benefits: supports heart health and healthy cholesterol levels, aids digestion, and helps regulate blood sugar due to its fiber content. Best eaten with the skin on for maximum fiber and antioxidants.

title: Banana
content: Banana is a widely eaten tropical fruit. Key nutrients: potassium, vitamin B6, vitamin C, and fiber. Health benefits: supports heart and muscle function through potassium, provides quick energy from natural sugars, and aids digestion. A convenient, easily digestible pre- or post-exercise snack.

title: Orange
content: Orange is a citrus fruit known for its high vitamin C content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, folate, and antioxidants (flavonoids). Health benefits: strengthens immune function, supports collagen production and skin health, and improves iron absorption from plant foods when eaten together.
keywords: Orange, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Mango
content: Mango is a sweet tropical fruit often called the 'king of fruits'. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, folate, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and aids digestion; best enjoyed in moderation due to its natural sugar content, especially for those managing blood sugar.
keywords: Mango, tropical fruit, vitamin a, fruit nutrition

title: Kiwi
content: Kiwi (kiwifruit) is a small fruit with green or gold flesh and edible black seeds. Key nutrients: exceptionally high in vitamin C (more per serving than an orange), vitamin K, fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to fiber and the enzyme actinidin, and supports healthy blood clotting via vitamin K.
keywords: Kiwi, kiwifruit, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Grapes
content: Grapes are small, sweet fruits that grow in clusters, available in green, red, and black varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants like resveratrol (concentrated in the skin, especially red/black grapes). Health benefits: antioxidants support heart health and may help reduce inflammation; best eaten in moderation due to natural sugar content.

title: Papaya
content: Papaya is a tropical fruit with orange flesh and black seeds. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, and the digestive enzyme papain. Health benefits: aids digestion (papain helps break down protein), supports immune function and eye health, and has anti-inflammatory properties.
keywords: Papaya, papain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Pineapple
content: Pineapple is a tropical fruit known for its sweet-tart flavor. Key nutrients: vitamin C, manganese, and the enzyme bromelain. Health benefits: bromelain has anti-inflammatory properties and aids protein digestion; vitamin C supports immune function and wound healing.
keywords: Pineapple, bromelain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Guava
content: Guava is a tropical fruit with edible skin and seeds. Key nutrients: extremely high in vitamin C (often more than citrus fruits), fiber, and lycopene (in pink-fleshed varieties). Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to high fiber content, and supports heart health through lycopene and potassium.
keywords: Guava, vitamin c, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Pomegranate
content: Pomegranate is a fruit with a thick skin containing many juicy, seeded arils. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and powerful antioxidants called punicalagins. Health benefits: supports heart health, has strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, and may support joint health.

title: Watermelon
content: Watermelon is a large, water-rich fruit (about 92% water). Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and the antioxidant lycopene. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, supports heart health and skin health through lycopene and vitamin C, and is naturally low in calories.

title: Strawberry
content: Strawberry is a sweet, red berry. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, manganese, folate, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function and skin health, and antioxidants may help reduce inflammation and support heart health.
keywords: Strawberry, berries, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Blueberry
content: Blueberry is a small, dark blue berry known for its high antioxidant content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and anthocyanins (the antioxidant giving them their color). Health benefits: supports brain health and memory, may reduce inflammation, and supports heart health.

title: Raspberry
content: Raspberry is a small, red or black aggregate fruit. Key nutrients: high in fiber, vitamin C, manganese, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health due to high fiber content, immune support from vitamin C, and antioxidants that may help reduce inflammation.

title: Muskmelon (Cantaloupe)
content: Muskmelon (cantaloupe) is a sweet, orange-fleshed melon. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and is hydrating with a high water content.

title: Pear
content: Pear is a sweet fruit with a grainy texture, eaten with or without the skin. Key nutrients: fiber (especially in the skin), vitamin C, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports digestive health and healthy cholesterol through its high fiber content, and provides steady energy from natural sugars.

title: Plum
content: Plum is a stone fruit with smooth skin, available in many varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health (dried plums/prunes are well known for this), supports bone health via vitamin K, and antioxidants support overall cell health.
keywords: Plum, prune, stone fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Peach
content: Peach is a sweet, fuzzy-skinned stone fruit. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and fiber. Health benefits: supports skin and immune health through vitamin C, and aids digestion through its fiber content.

title: Apricot
content: Apricot is a small, orange stone fruit, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health through its high vitamin A (beta-carotene) content, and supports heart health and digestion.
keywords: Apricot, stone fruit, beta-carotene, fruit nutrition

title: Fig
content: Fig is a soft, sweet fruit with a fiber-rich, seeded interior, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: fiber, calcium, potassium, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health and, notably, provides more calcium than many other fruits, supporting bone health.

title: Lemon and Lime
content: Lemon and lime are tart citrus fruits, usually used for their juice and zest rather than eaten whole. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, along with citric acid and flavonoid antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function, improves iron absorption from plant foods when added to meals, and adds flavor to water/dishes without added sugar.
keywords: Lemon, lime, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Avocado
content: Avocado is a nutrient-dense fruit known for its creamy texture and healthy fat content. Key nutrients: monounsaturated (heart-healthy) fats, fiber, potassium, vitamin E, and folate. Health benefits: supports heart health through healthy fats, aids nutrient absorption of fat-soluble vitamins from other foods, and supports satiety due to its fat and fiber content.
keywords: Avocado, healthy fats, monounsaturated fat, fruit nutrition

title: Coconut
content: Coconut is a fruit whose flesh, water, and oil are all commonly consumed. Key nutrients: coconut flesh is high in fiber and medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs); coconut water is rich in electrolytes like potassium. Health benefits: coconut water supports hydration and electrolyte balance; coconut flesh provides fiber, though it is also high in saturated fat, so moderation is advised.
keywords: Coconut, coconut water, MCT, fruit nutrition

title: Spinach
content: Spinach is a leafy green vegetable. Key nutrients: iron, vitamin K, vitamin A, folate, and antioxidants (lutein). Health benefits: supports healthy blood (iron and folate for red blood cell production), bone health via vitamin K, and eye health via lutein and vitamin A. Pairing with vitamin C rich foods improves iron absorption.
keywords: Spinach, leafy greens, iron, vegetable nutrition

title: Broccoli
content: Broccoli is a cruciferous vegetable with edible flower buds and stalk. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and sulforaphane (a compound linked to cancer-protective effects). Health benefits: strong immune and bone support, aids digestion, and its sulforaphane content is associated with reduced cancer risk in research.
keywords: Broccoli, cruciferous vegetable, sulforaphane, vegetable nutrition

title: Carrot
content: Carrot is a root vegetable, typically orange, though also found in purple, yellow, and white varieties. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (converted to vitamin A), fiber, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, and aids digestion through fiber.
keywords: Carrot, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

title: Tomato
content: Tomato is botanically a fruit but commonly used as a vegetable. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium, folate, and lycopene (an antioxidant more available when tomatoes are cooked). Health benefits: supports heart health through lycopene and potassium, and immune function through vitamin C.

title: Potato
content: Potato is a starchy root vegetable and a staple food in many cuisines. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium (more than a banana), vitamin B6, and fiber (especially in the skin). Health benefits: provides energy through carbohydrates, supports heart function via potassium; preparation method (baked/boiled vs. fried) significantly affects its overall healthfulness.
keywords: Potato, starchy vegetable, potassium, vegetable nutrition

title: Onion
content: Onion is a pungent bulb vegetable used widely in cooking. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, and antioxidants (quercetin) along with prebiotic fibers. Health benefits: quercetin has anti-inflammatory properties, and its prebiotic fiber supports healthy gut bacteria.

title: Cucumber
content: Cucumber is a high-water-content vegetable, often eaten raw. Key nutrients: mostly water (about 95%), with some vitamin K and potassium. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, low in calories, and its fiber (especially the skin) supports digestion.
keywords: Cucumber, hydration, low calorie, vegetable nutrition

title: Cauliflower
content: Cauliflower is a cruciferous vegetable, related to broccoli and cabbage. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and choline. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and is a popular low-carbohydrate substitute for grains like rice in many diets.
keywords: Cauliflower, cruciferous vegetable, low carb, vegetable nutrition

title: Cabbage
content: Cabbage is a leafy cruciferous vegetable, available in green, red/purple, and savoy varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and fiber. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and fermented cabbage (like sauerkraut/kimchi) also provides probiotics for gut health.
keywords: Cabbage, cruciferous vegetable, sauerkraut, vegetable nutrition

title: Bell Pepper (Capsicum)
content: Bell pepper (capsicum) is a sweet pepper available in green, red, yellow, and orange varieties. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C (red peppers have more than oranges), vitamin A, and antioxidants. Health benefits: strong immune support, supports eye health, and adds color/nutrients to meals with few calories.
keywords: Bell pepper, capsicum, vitamin c, vegetable nutrition

title: Sweet Potato
content: Sweet potato is a starchy root vegetable with orange, purple, or white flesh. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (vitamin A), fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, aids digestion via fiber, and has a lower glycemic impact than regular potatoes when boiled.
keywords: Sweet potato, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

title: Beetroot
content: Beetroot is a deep red-purple root vegetable. Key nutrients: folate, potassium, fiber, and nitrates (natural compounds that convert to nitric oxide in the body). Health benefits: nitrates may support healthy blood pressure and exercise performance, and folate supports red blood cell production.

title: Classic Chocolate Milkshake
content: Recipe: Classic Chocolate Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and chocolate syrup until smooth and creamy. Calories: 320. Protein: 8g. Calcium: 280mg (notably rich). A classic indulgent treat; best enjoyed in moderation due to added sugar content.
keywords: Chocolate milkshake, milkshake recipe, dessert drink, chocolate

title: Strawberry Milkshake
content: Recipe: Strawberry Milkshake. Blend fresh strawberries, milk, and vanilla ice cream until smooth. Calories: 260. Protein: 7g. Vitamin C: 28mg (notably rich). Fresh strawberries add real vitamin C and antioxidants to this classic fruity shake.
keywords: Strawberry milkshake, fruit milkshake, dessert drink

title: Banana Peanut Butter Protein Milkshake
content: Recipe: Banana Peanut Butter Protein Milkshake. Blend banana, milk, peanut butter, and a scoop of protein powder until smooth. Calories: 380. Protein: 24g (notably rich). Potassium: 550mg (notably rich). A high-protein, potassium-rich milkshake, great as a post-workout recovery drink or a filling breakfast.
keywords: Banana milkshake, protein milkshake, peanut butter, post workout, high protein

title: Mango Milkshake
content: Recipe: Mango Milkshake. Blend ripe mango, milk, and a little sugar or honey until smooth and creamy. Calories: 250. Protein: 6g. Vitamin A: 45% RDA (notably rich). Vitamin C: 20mg. A tropical, vitamin-A-rich shake that's a popular summer favorite.
keywords: Mango milkshake, fruit milkshake, tropical drink

title: Vanilla Milkshake
content: Recipe: Vanilla Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and a splash of vanilla extract until thick and smooth. Calories: 300. Protein: 8g. Calcium: 270mg (notably rich). A simple, classic milkshake base that pairs well with any mix-in (fruit, cocoa, nut butter).
keywords: Vanilla milkshake, classic milkshake, dessert drink

title: Protein Power Milkshake
content: Recipe: Protein Power Milkshake. Blend milk (or plant milk), a scoop of whey or plant protein powder, a banana, and ice until smooth. Calories: 280. Protein: 30g (notably rich). Calcium: 300mg (notably rich). Designed as a high-protein recovery drink for muscle repair after exercise, or a quick high-protein breakfast.
keywords: Protein milkshake, muscle recovery, high protein, fitness drink, post workout

title: Oreo Cookies and Cream Milkshake
content: Recipe: Oreo Cookies and Cream Milkshake. Blend milk, vanilla ice cream, and crushed Oreo cookies until thick and smooth. Calories: 420. Protein: 8g. An indulgent dessert-style milkshake best enjoyed occasionally as a treat rather than a regular drink.
keywords: Oreo milkshake, cookies and cream, dessert drink

title: Mocha Coffee Milkshake
content: Recipe: Mocha Coffee Milkshake. Blend cold coffee or espresso, milk, chocolate syrup, and vanilla ice cream until smooth. Calories: 310. Protein: 7g. A caffeinated dessert drink combining coffee and chocolate flavors; contains caffeine, so best enjoyed earlier in the day.
keywords: Mocha milkshake, coffee milkshake, caffeine drink

title: Mixed Berry Milkshake
content: Recipe: Mixed Berry Milkshake. Blend a mix of strawberries, blueberries, and raspberries with milk and a little honey until smooth. Calories: 230. Protein: 6g. Vitamin C: 35mg (notably rich). A naturally antioxidant-rich shake from the combined berries, with less added sugar than ice-cream-based shakes.
keywords: Berry milkshake, mixed berry, antioxidant drink, fruit milkshake

title: Avocado Banana Milkshake
content: Recipe: Avocado Banana Milkshake. Blend avocado, banana, milk, and a touch of honey until creamy and smooth. Calories: 340. Protein: 7g. Potassium: 700mg (notably rich). A creamy, nutrient-dense shake rich in healthy monounsaturated fats and potassium, without needing ice cream for its thick texture.
keywords: Avocado milkshake, banana milkshake, healthy fats, potassium

title: Eggs
content: Eggs are a nutrient-dense whole food and one of the most complete protein sources available. Key nutrients: high-quality complete protein (all 9 essential amino acids), vitamin B12, vitamin D, choline, selenium, and riboflavin; the yolk also contains lutein and zeaxanthin, antioxidants that support eye health. Health benefits: supports muscle maintenance and growth, choline supports brain and liver health, and eggs are an affordable, versatile source of high-quality protein. Modern nutrition research shows dietary cholesterol from eggs has a much smaller effect on blood cholesterol for most people than once believed, so moderate egg consumption (about 1 egg/day) fits well within a healthy diet for most adults.
keywords: Eggs, egg nutrition, complete protein, choline, protein source

title: Scrambled Eggs
content: Recipe: Scrambled Eggs. Whisk eggs with a splash of milk, season with salt and pepper, and cook gently over low heat, stirring continuously until soft and creamy. Calories: 180. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A quick, high-protein breakfast staple; add vegetables like spinach or tomato for extra nutrients.
keywords: Scrambled eggs, egg recipe, breakfast, high protein

title: Boiled Eggs (Hard and Soft-Boiled)
content: Recipe: Boiled Eggs. Place eggs in a pot of water, bring to a boil, then simmer 6-7 minutes for soft-boiled or 10-12 minutes for hard-boiled, then cool in cold water before peeling. Calories: 78 (per egg). Protein: 6g (notably rich). One of the simplest, most portable high-protein snacks, with no added fat needed.
keywords: Boiled eggs, hard boiled egg, soft boiled egg, egg recipe, snack

title: Vegetable Omelette
content: Recipe: Vegetable Omelette. Whisk eggs, pour into a hot pan, and fill with sauteed onions, tomatoes, bell peppers, and spinach; fold in half once set. Calories: 220. Protein: 15g (notably rich). Vitamin C: 18mg. A filling breakfast combining protein with vegetable vitamins and fiber.
keywords: Omelette, vegetable omelette, egg recipe, breakfast

title: Egg Fried Rice
content: Recipe: Egg Fried Rice. Scramble eggs in a hot wok, then stir-fry with cooked rice, chopped vegetables (carrots, peas, spring onion), and soy sauce until well combined. Calories: 340. Protein: 12g. A quick, balanced one-pan meal combining protein, carbohydrates, and vegetables.
keywords: Egg fried rice, egg recipe, rice dish

title: Egg Curry
content: Recipe: Egg Curry. Simmer hard-boiled eggs in a spiced onion-tomato gravy (ginger, garlic, turmeric, chili, garam masala) until the flavors soak in. Calories: 280. Protein: 14g (notably rich). A protein-rich Indian-style curry, usually served with rice or roti.
keywords: Egg curry, Indian egg recipe, curry

title: Egg Bhurji
content: Recipe: Egg Bhurji. An Indian-style spiced scrambled egg dish - whisked eggs cooked with sauteed onions, tomatoes, green chilies, and turmeric until fluffy and well combined. Calories: 210. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A quick, flavorful, high-protein breakfast or dinner dish, popular across India.
keywords: Egg bhurji, Indian egg recipe, scrambled egg, spiced eggs

title: Egg Salad
content: Recipe: Egg Salad. Mash hard-boiled eggs with a little mayonnaise or yogurt, mustard, and chopped celery/onion; season with salt and pepper. Calories: 250. Protein: 13g (notably rich). A protein-rich salad, great in a sandwich or on its own with greens; using yogurt instead of mayonnaise lightens it up.
keywords: Egg salad, egg recipe, protein salad

title: Poached Eggs
content: Recipe: Poached Eggs. Crack an egg into a small cup, then gently slide it into barely simmering water with a splash of vinegar; cook 3-4 minutes until the white sets but the yolk stays runny. Calories: 72 (per egg). Protein: 6g (notably rich). A classic, no-added-fat way to prepare eggs, often served over toast or greens (as in eggs Benedict-style dishes).

title: Egg Sandwich
content: Recipe: Egg Sandwich. Layer a fried or scrambled egg between whole-grain bread with lettuce, tomato, and a light spread of mayonnaise or avocado. Calories: 300. Protein: 16g (notably rich). Fiber: 4g. A balanced, portable breakfast or lunch combining protein, whole grains, and vegetables.
keywords: Egg sandwich, egg recipe, breakfast sandwich
